symbols: Belshazzar with raised golden cup — drinking from the sacred Temple vessels in sacrilege
harp and musicians — the decadent revelry of Babylon's court
dancers — the sensuous excess of the feast
tall crown/headdress — Babylonian royal power about to be judged
dark atmospheric lighting — the approaching doom and judgment
reclining figures — the indolent luxury of a kingdom about to fall
description: A dramatic painting depicting a lavish Babylonian feast scene. At the right, a figure wearing a tall ornate crown or headdress and golden-orange robes stands holding a raised golden cup. Before him, musicians and reclining guests fill the scene — a figure plays a large harp, dancers perform, and various courtiers recline on couches. The atmosphere is dark and moody with warm, flickering light illuminating the figures. The scene conveys opulent decadence and revelry.
theological_description: The Feast of Belshazzar (Daniel 5) represents one of Scripture's most dramatic moments of divine judgment. King Belshazzar 'made a great feast for a thousand of his lords and drank wine in front of the thousand' (Daniel 5:1). In his arrogance, he commanded that the gold and silver vessels taken from the Temple in Jerusalem be brought so he and his guests could drink from them — a deliberate sacrilege against the God of Israel. As they drank wine and praised 'the gods of gold and silver, bronze, iron, wood, and stone' (Daniel 5:4), a mysterious hand appeared and wrote on the wall: MENE, MENE, TEKEL, UPHARSIN. Daniel interpreted: 'God has numbered the days of your kingdom and brought it to an end; you have been weighed in the balances and found wanting; your kingdom is divided and given to the Medes and Persians' (Daniel 5:26-28). That very night Belshazzar was slain. The theological message is stark: worldly power that defies God and desecrates what is holy will be judged swiftly and completely. This pattern echoes through Revelation: 'Fallen, fallen is Babylon the great!' (Revelation 18:2).

scripture_refs.reason: Belshazzar's feast and the desecration of the Temple vessels — the event depicted
MENE MENE TEKEL UPHARSIN — the divine verdict on Belshazzar's kingdom
That very night Belshazzar was slain — the immediate fulfillment of judgment
Fallen, fallen is Babylon the great — the eschatological echo of Belshazzar's downfall
Pride goes before destruction — the principle demonstrated in Belshazzar's arrogant feast
